factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds like <250 ms, <500 ms, and >1 s, but it does not state the specific HCI timing guidelines of ~0.1 s as instantaneous, ~1 s preserving thought, and >10 s losing attention. The 10-second attention-loss threshold is not mentioned, nor is the 0.1 s/1 s framing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends targeting suggestion latency at “<250 ms” and mentions “~100–250 ms” as ideal, but it does not state that inline suggestions should target a 150–300 ms first-render window specifically to preserve flow state. The exact 150–300 ms range and “first render” framing are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives explicit latency ranges for multi-line suggestions (e.g., Tabnine under 500 ms, CodeWhisperer 500 ms–1 s) and recommends keeping multi-line/large-block suggestions under 500 ms. It also discusses presentation considerations like avoiding clutter/overlapping prompts and using adaptive timing, which function as boundaries/visual affordances.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives CodeWhisperer comment-to-code latency as “500 ms–1 s” and discusses disruption above 1 second, but it does not state that 0.5–2 seconds is tolerable specifically when paired with streaming or progress indicators. It also never mentions streaming or progress indicators as a mitigation for comment-to-code latency.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends shifting to on-demand/manual triggering for debugging and complex tasks, and it also suggests adaptive timing to minimize disruption when latency is higher. This effectively covers using on-demand mode when generation may exceed timing budgets.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends shifting to on-demand/manual triggers for debugging and using proactive suggestions for routine coding and feature development, explicitly stating this in the best-practices summary and recommendations section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response contrasts debugging vs feature development in terms of interruption sensitivity and acceptance, but it does not state specific edit-run cycle durations (e.g., ~1 minute for debugging vs ~3 minutes for feature development). No approximate minute-level cycle times are provided anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that developers with 2–5 years experience have “higher acceptance rates of AI suggestions,” while developers with 10+ years experience have “acceptance rates [that] are lower,” directly asserting higher acceptance among less-experienced developers than 10+ year developers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts GitHub Copilot as inline suggestions, Tabnine as multi-line predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer as comment-to-code generation in the latency and workflow sections.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses acceptance rates such as “65–80%” for feature development but does not report acceptance rates specifically in the 25–35% range, nor does it tie that range to production deployment. The required 25–35% production acceptance-rate metric is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses acceptance rates in general terms (e.g., “65–80%”) but does not report any of the specific documented metrics required: Copilot 10–15% acceptance gain, CodeWhisperer 50–60% faster, or JetBrains 40–50% acceptance increase. None of those ranges or equivalent claims appear anywhere in the text.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses that higher acceptance rates occur with timely proactive suggestions but also highlights risks like hallucinated or “almost-right” code, elevated verification burdens, and potential impacts on maintainability and quality. This explicitly frames a trade-off between optimizing acceptance/adoption and preserving code quality/long-term outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response synthesizes a decision framework by recommending proactive suggestions for routine/feature work and junior developers, while shifting to on-demand/manual triggering for debugging, complex changes, and senior developers. It also adds conditional guidance based on latency thresholds and adaptive timing tied to task context and user behavior.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes some measured outcomes like “acceptance rates as high as 65–80%” and mentions vendor deployment options (e.g., Tabnine local/on-prem). However, it does not clearly extract vendor-specific deployment techniques paired with concrete measured outcomes (e.g., “filter X increased acceptance by +50%”) tied to specific deployment/UI techniques.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations for 2–5 year versus 10+ year developers and gives specific strategy guidance by cohort (e.g., proactive suggestions and explanations for juniors, on-demand/manual triggers and minimal explanations for seniors), including task-based timing recommendations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response recommends adapting suggestion mode based on context (proactive for routine coding, on-demand/manual triggers for debugging and complex changes) and mentions configurability of trigger modes and frequency. However, it does not explicitly specify escalation paths when timing budgets are exceeded (e.g., switching from streaming to progress indicators to on-demand fallback).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response contrasts calibrated trust (e.g., “reviewer-first,” confidence/provenance indicators, optional explanations) with risks of over-reliance (e.g., “blind acceptance,” “over-reliance can lead to confusion”). It also proposes concrete UI patterns like opt-in/toggleable explanations, on-demand triggers vs proactive suggestions, and adaptive timing/throttling by task context.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly identifies multiple research gaps (e.g., lack of experience-segmented ms-level latency studies and explanation effectiveness data) and recommends adaptive timing plus configurable, opt-in explanations and deployment options—i.e., instrumentation/configurability to handle uncertainty.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes that proactive suggestions can disrupt flow, especially during debugging and for senior developers, and recommends adaptive or on-demand triggering to minimize interruption costs. It explicitly discusses interruption research and flow-state disruption rather than presenting proactive suggestions as universally beneficial.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses explicit timing terminology and units, citing latencies like ~200 ms, 100 ms, <250 ms, <500 ms, and 500 ms–1 s, and ties these to flow preservation and disruption thresholds.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response compares Copilot, Tabnine, and CodeWhisperer in separate tool-specific sections and repeatedly cross-cuts the discussion by experience level (2–5 vs 10+ years) and by task type (debugging vs feature development) when discussing latency, acceptance, and trust.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, research-synthesis style with structured sections, comparative claims, and citations, and it does not include personal anecdotes. It avoids overt promotional or sales-oriented language, focusing on findings, gaps, and recommendations in an objective tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends major sections with concrete, research-linked design recommendations (e.g., latency targets <250 ms/<500 ms, proactive vs on-demand by task and seniority, and opt-in explanations), especially in the “Summary of Best Practices and Recommendations” and “Conclusion” sections.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is written primarily as prose paragraphs with narrative sections and bullet lists, rather than using tables or a labeled side-by-side comparison format for Copilot vs Tabnine vs CodeWhisperer. While it has headings, it does not present parallel, tool-by-tool sections with consistent fields or a comparative table.

citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research from arXiv on AI code suggestion timing and debugging (e.g., sources [6], [9], and [11] are arXiv links), satisfying the requirement to cite research from arXiv/ACM/IEEE/ResearchGate.

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)

citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly frames timing and latency claims as supported by studies/telemetry and provides a sources section with citations (e.g., Microsoft, JetBrains, arXiv, Microsoft Research) rather than relying on personal anecdotes or team experiences.

citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a mix of sources including arXiv papers, Microsoft Research, JetBrains and Microsoft blogs, and other web articles, rather than relying solely on personal blogs, forums, or social media.
